questions.scenario: You need to store the facility name and print a greeting. Spaces in variable assignment are fatal.
questions.question: Assign 'Reactor Block A' to FACILITY and print: 'Welcome to Reactor Block A'.
questions.description: Variable assignment in bash has NO spaces around =. x = 1 is a syntax error (bash tries to run x as a command). x=1 works. Always quote values with spaces.
hints: Quote the value since it has spaces
Run: FACILITY='Reactor Block A' && echo "Welcome to $FACILITY"

questions.scenario: You need to understand the difference between double and single quotes. Double quotes expand variables; single quotes don't.
questions.question: Print the literal text '$USER' (not the expanded username) using single quotes.
questions.description: Single quotes preserve everything literally — no variable expansion, no command substitution, no escape sequences. Double quotes allow $var expansion and $(cmd) substitution. Use single quotes when you want raw text.
use_cases: Write configs containing literal dollar signs
Prevent accidental variable expansion in output

questions.scenario: A script has DIR='' (empty). Running rm -rf $DIR/* unquoted deletes the current directory. Prove it by showing what unquoted empty var expands to.
questions.question: Show that an unquoted empty variable disappears: set EMPTY='' and echo the result of echo "arg1" $EMPTY "arg2".
questions.description: Unquoted empty variables vanish entirely — bash removes them from the argument list. This is why rm -rf $dir/* with empty dir becomes rm -rf /* (delete everything). Always quote variables that could be empty.
use_cases: Understand the root cause of the rm -rf disaster pattern
Debug scripts with unexpected argument counts
Know why $# changes with unquoted empty vars
hints: Set EMPTY='' then use it unquoted between two args
The empty var should simply disappear from the output

questions.scenario: IFS (Internal Field Separator) controls how bash splits words. Show what happens when IFS is set to a comma.
questions.question: Set IFS=',' and use read to split 'alice,bob,charlie' into three variables A, B, C. Print all three.
questions.description: IFS determines what characters bash treats as word separators. Changing it is powerful but dangerous — always restore it or use a subshell. read uses IFS to split input into variables.
use_cases: Parse CSV data line by line
hints: Set IFS=',' then use read A B C <<< 'alice,bob,charlie'
Run: IFS=',' read A B C <<< 'alice,bob,charlie' && echo "$A $B $C"

validation.command: IFS=',' read A B C <<< 'alice,bob,charlie' && echo "$A $B $C"

questions.scenario: A filename contains spaces: 'reactor log.txt'. Show that unquoted variables break when filenames have spaces.
questions.question: Assign 'reactor log.txt' to FILE and show that echo $FILE produces two words but echo "$FILE" produces one.
questions.description: Quoting "$var" prevents word splitting and glob expansion. This is THE most important bash quoting rule. Any variable that could contain spaces, tabs, or glob characters must be double-quoted at the point of use.
use_cases: Prevent glob expansion of stored patterns
Pass multi-word arguments correctly to commands
hints: Set FILE='reactor log.txt', try both echo $FILE and echo "$FILE"
Run: FILE='reactor log.txt' && echo $FILE && echo '---' && echo "$FILE"
